I've written letters. I wrote a letter on a diet promotion that brought in, I think, forty million dollars more recently than the coat of arms thing. It's important to realize what you were playing for. And the next note I want you to take is I want you to write this one in big capital letters because I want you to understand what business you're in and I never want you to forget this. You're not in the mail order business, you're not in the direct mail business, you're not in the dentist business, you're not in the hardware business, you are in the arithmetic business. Write that down. You are in the arithmetic business. And if you don't get the arithmetic right, everything else is going to crumble around you. I have people contact me or come to seminars with something to sell and I'll ask them to describe it for me. One of the more recent guys is a guy, Jean Donald, Frank called me, that guy we drove down to, what the, but, I refer him to you. What this guy has now is a little tag that, I think you write your kid's name on it, is that it? The kid's phone. Well, yeah, I want you to listen to this. He wants to sell this by direct mail and it cost about five bucks for a person to buy one of these, okay, through direct mail. Now, what's he buying for? Who knows? Let's say that he pays nothing for them, okay? Nothing. Let us now talk about him getting his 2% response. See, it's $5 over here and he makes, he sells 20 of them. That's 100 bucks. He lost $400. To make a profit, to break even, he would have to sell 100 of these on a mailing list of a thousand. How many people out there do you think are hungry for a little tag if they can put their kid's name on it? What I'm saying is, it's doomed. It's not even, it's not even possible. So you've got to know that the arithmetic tells you that it's even possible, right? Okay, now, I believe that the best way of learning something is by apprenticeship. I think there is nothing that you can do that will get you the information and the education you need that works better than working right with somebody who is a master at what they're doing. I try to not take clients anymore. I've taken two in the last year. My fee is $30,000 upfront plus 20% of profit. And what I want to do here is I want to write a direct mail promotion for one of you free. I'm not going to charge the $30,000 and I'm not going to charge any percentage of the profit. Is there anybody that would want me to do that has a product that would like for me to do that for them? I'm going to do it right for you. Now, first of all, we're going to have to know a little bit. I'm going to give myself the best odds possible. Do you understand that? Now, let me talk to you about that for a moment. What did I just say? Write it down. Give yourself the best shot possible. Are you looking for challenge? Anybody in here looking for challenges? I mean, we got some lesbian bars here on the beach. I mean, I got it. You got it. Thank God for editing rooms. So what that means, let's suppose that one of you has a pill that will make people look old and wrinkled and fat. And another of you has a pill that will make people look young and beautiful and slim. Which one of those do you think I'm going to write the letter for? Okay? Now, there's a real good point there, though. It is tough enough to do this under utopian conditions. So why not do it under the best conditions you can have? I've actually had people call me and say, I hear you're the best copywriter in the world. You're such a creative guy. Everybody tells me I've got a real challenge for you. My company manufactures a product called Inglue Refrigerators. There's little portable refrigerators that you can carry around. And we thought if we could get some advertising guy to write a campaign that would sell them to actual Eskimos living in Alaska that we could dramatize at, blah, blah, blah. And I'd say, you know, I don't need any more challenge. I have enough challenge paying the rent, you know? So if you have some freedom of choice, why would someone, for example, choose to be an insurance salesman? Why would they? I mean, how many of you have ever been overjoyed to buy insurance? Nobody. I mean, your face doesn't light up when a guy comes out. Why do people, you know, you never see the movie? How many of you have seen the movie The Graduate? Have you seen that? And, you know, there's hardly anything more straight-laced than a Mormon. And they were always... What I mean by a passion index, you all do various things, but some of them you care more about than others. Take bowlers and golfers. Bowlers care about what they're doing, but not so much. You know, they wear nylon shirts and they get drunk and they both. Golfers are insane. It is very, very difficult to write a promotion to a golfer, a good golfing list that will fail if you have anything, you know, if you know anything at all about what you are doing. Okay? Now, weight loss, if you hit, might be a bigger one, but you might not. There's a lot more competition there. So what we're going to do is we're going to make this man a fortune before we leave today. Well, before we leave this weekend. And we are going to do that in a way that you, all of the rest of you that I am not writing for, can do the same thing. And all of you being trained a little bit now about being associative thinkers are not going to say, well, I sell templates for CD-ROMs, that doesn't apply to me. You're not going to say that to me, are you? You're going to catch this, right? Okay. All right. Now, what we're going to write again, I'm not going to have you write as much as I did at the last seminar. And I'm going to have you write something and then you are going to begin learning. Now, the two letters that you have to your mother, that is the beginning if you don't already have one of what we refer in the business as a swipe file. Okay? And you want to save those letters, you want to save this next one you're going to write, you want to save others, you want a file. Now, believe it or not, how long do you think it would take me to write a winning letter for that guy if I didn't have to teach you how to do it, Paul, during the course of this seminar for his putter? That's about right. Probably about 90 minutes with polish. With making it perfect, it would take me about 90 minutes to make it perfect. Thanks. Would you read the last sentence? I can't remember where it was. This drawing along with other information about the name, it's pretty tough to track it out. Okay, new paragraph, that means we do a space and we indent five pages. Or five, whatever, be technical, you know what I mean. Five characters, whatever. The bottom half of the report tells the story of the very old and distinguished family name of McDonald. Period. It tells what the name means, comma, and the name of the family name. Period. Is it ten altogether or ten plus? It tells what the name means, comma, it's origin, comma, O-R-I-G-I-N. The original family motto, comma. Place in history, no comma. And about famous people who share it. Period. I'll read it again. It tells what the name means, comma, it's origin, comma, the original family motto, comma. It's place in history and about famous people who share it, period. A word about commas. I determine where commas go by reading my copy aloud. And when I come to a natural pause, that's where I put my comma. There may be English teachers who say that's not grammatically correct, but I think it works to communicate effectively. There is not a comma after the last, between the next to the last and the last thing that you are itemizing. It's place in history and about famous people who share it. There's no comma after history because that is replaced by the conjunctive word and. That's just a little minor thing, but okay. New sentence, same paragraph. The top half has a large comma. Beautiful reproduction of an artist drawing. There is that that is a possessive. So you'll put what's that called before the S. I knew that. So it's apostrophe s, an artist's drawing of the earliest known coat of arms. Coat of arms is a hyphenated word for the name of McDonald period. The name of McDonald. Yes. I'm presuming this is mc and not m a c. It is actually m a c d o and in this example, they do it both ways. This is m a c. This entire report. Yes. Is documented comma. Authentic. No comma. And printed. On parchment like paper. Now parchment like. Is a hyphenated word. If you will think about that without the hyphen, you will see that it does not work. Then it would be parchment like paper. It doesn't doesn't quite work. So it's parchment like paper suitable for framing period. Drop down a space because we're going down to a new paragraph. We are going to indent five spaces. The report. So delighted our friends. That we have had. A few extra copies. Made in order. To share this information. Other people. Of the same name. Period. The report so delighted our friends. That we have had. A few extra copies made. In order to share. This information. With other people. Of the same name. Period. Drop a space. New paragraph. Which means that we indent five spaces. Framed comma. Reports. Make distinctive wall decorations. And they are. Great gifts. For relatives. Period. We'll pause for a moment. How long do you think would you guys some of you know so if you know don't tell the answer how long do you think it took me to write this letter. When I originally wrote it. Took me one and a half years took me 18 months to write this letter. This was my first successful sales letter. And. The thing is is this. Letter is. Packed. Psychology that is not apparent to the naked eye or the naked mind. The sentence that you just read for example. Has two very important pieces of psychology in it. First it tells you. What you could do with it. Which is put it on your wall. And later research proved that the number one thing that people were buying here and it's always important to know what they are buying. They're buying wall hanging recognition. The second thing is it says makes great gifts for relatives. So that's a suggestion as to why they should buy more. And also. Many times it is easier for someone to buy something under the guise of that gift being for someone else when you really want it yourself. I'm going to buy his book on speed seduction for my son bond. No but. A real good example of that is fathers who buy electric trains for their kids. You know you always want electric train now there's a reason to get one. So there's a lot of psychology and everywhere if you if you've got your psychology wrong. It doesn't you can't get your words right. It doesn't make any difference. Okay. Same paragraph new sentence. It should be remembered. That we have not traced. Anyone's. And that has an apostrophe with it because it's possessive. Individual family tree. But have researched back. Through several centuries. To find out. About. The earliest. The word earliest is underlined. Now sometimes when I write letters I underline words and sometimes I put them in italics. And I use a different mental reference to make that choice. But the the reference I use this to when it's underlined is when I want to make a point like a punch to find out about the earliest. That's underlined. Okay. To find out about the earliest people named McDonald. Period. New paragraph. Which means there's a space. Which means it's indented five spaces. All we are asking for them. Is enough. To cover the added expense. Of having. The extra copies. Printed. And mail. Period. Parentheses. See. Below. Period. Close parentheses. If you. See below is inside the parentheses. See below and the period are both inside the parentheses. By the way, I just this is a variation of the letter I did not write. I did not write that C below. I just picked that out. You know, there's been this. This letter has been altered over the centuries sort of. And had I done that. I would. The real way that that should be done is if there should be no period in it. I wrote it literally as it is here and there should be it should be perenned. See below. Close perrens. And there should be no capital letters in there all either. That's a difference that whispers not one that screams same paragraph new sentence. If you are interested comma. Please let us know. Right away. As our supply. Is pretty slim. Period. Pretty slim. That's what it said in the original version of this letter. Some alphabet agency in Ohio. Objected to this letter. And sent one of the mad dog inspectors to bath to talk to Dennis Haslinger and myself. And when he found out that we really were creating the reports really were sending them out and so on and so forth. He of course had to justify his existence and change something. So he insisted that we so that he would not lose face. Change the phrase pretty slim to limited because our supplies are limited. And we had to destroy a quarter million pieces of mail on a truck to make that change. In case any of you are not in business and you think that example is outrageous or far fetched. The real truth is it's commonplace. So I don't know what the point of telling you that was. But there was. No. Matter of fact they probably put it back to pretty slim. You know I mean the guy had justified all the expense that his parents paid for him to get his PhD and his bullshit job with the government and made a really important contribution to society in our future. Same paragraph. Next sentence. Just verify. That we have your correct name and address. Now I would like to say something about that if I were writing that letter today. The word that would be eliminated. Terry and I go through everything I write and look for what we call STs which are superfluous that's and almost every writer even the Pulitzer Prize winners have you can eliminate not all but nearly every word that and make the sentence flow better. Because it would then read just verify we have your correct name and address. But that's not what it does read so just verify that we have your correct name and address. And send the correct amount. In cash or check. For the number. Of reports you want. Period. We all that's a contraction. And we could have written we will but it says real. And why would I use the contraction instead of we will. Yeah that's exactly right it's kind of friendly. We'll send them promptly. By return mail. Period. The salutation is the word sincerely followed by a comma sincerely comma. The letter was signed by my wife at that time Nancy L. Halbert. Has a PS. Capital P. Period. Capital S. Period. When you do PS's they are indented and the second third and all the rest of the sentences only come back out to the indentation. PS. If you are ordering only one report. Comma. Send to T. W. O. Clarity is supremely important. And this makes I do this with prices a lot it's only twelve dollars T. W. E. L. B. E. Dollars parentheses you know and then I put it in numeric just just just a little bit of clarity. Period. Additional reports. Ordered at the same time. And sent to the same address. Our one dollar each. Period. One dollar. Yes. One dollar. Please make checks payable to me. Comma. Nancy L. Halbert. Period. You got it. Did you have it. What's your name. No your name. No no no the lady with the red hair. Lucia do you have that written. Okay. Now I'm going to have Lucia the redheaded lady here. Stand up please and just read that letter back what you every word of it from the beginning over here to over there. Right the address everything. I wrote that. Can I give her my mic. Hold a second we'll have you start again and I just handed my mic. Okay just go ahead he doesn't need. I just got. We've had an artist recreated code of arms exactly as described in the ancient record this drawing along with other information about. The bottom half of the report tells the story of the very old and distinguished family name of McDonald's it tells what the name means it's origin the original family motto in place in history and about things people who share it. The top half has a large beautiful reproduction of an artist's drawing of the earliest known code of arms for the name of McDonald's this entire report is documented authentic and printed on parchment like paper suitable for framing. The report so delighted our friends that we have a few of them today in order to share this information with other people of the same name. Strange these reports make distinctive wall decorations and they are great gifts for relatives. It should be remembered that we have not traced anyone's individual family tree but have researched back for several centuries to find out about the earliest people named McDonald's. All we are asking for then is enough to keep the daddy's friends and family the extra copy printed and mailed. If you are interested please let us know right away that our supply is pretty slim. Just verify that we have your correct name and address and send us correct amounts in cash or check for the number of reports you want. We'll send them promptly by return mail. I want you to read one of those sentences twice again and it's just I want you to read it once just verify that we have your correct name and address etc etc. And then read it again without putting the word that in there. OK. Just verify that we have your correct name and address and send the correct amount in cash or check for the number of reports you want now without the word. Just verify we have your correct name and address and send the correct amount in cash or check for the number of reports. Thank you. We're not going to do a lot more of this in this seminar because that would be disruptive and it would appear to be dull and repetitive work. But I want to explain what you just did. Now there's one last piece of psychology in the letter that I want to explain. It said additional reports ordered at the same time and sent to the same place are only a dollar. There's psychology there. If you order more than one you get a better deal and not only that there's a reason that you get the better deal because we're sending it at the same time to the same place. Now you now have a third letter for your swipe file that has been written in your own handwriting. Now I'm going to tell you something that that just is astonishing to me. The first when I first announced this seminar the first one a couple of months ago seems like every copywriter in the world wanted to come. And we had some really good copywriters here and we had some that wish they were really good. And one of them said to John Carlton do you guys really write that stuff out in your own handwriting. What a stupid question. Yeah we do. Yeah we really really do that. Thomas Hall who was my main mentor will take a sales letter that he admires that admires because it works well for someone else and will write it in his own handwriting 10 20 30 40 times. Yes I do do that. The way it's really done is often not the way you would think it is done. People think that is the sophistication that makes the difference. It's that's really not the case. The things that work are so simple a better word for them would be corny. Now the reason you want to write out letters in your own handwriting is because you get a neural imprint. You have just written by the way just think about you have just written the most successful sales letter in history. You have literally just written it and you now have a neural imprint on your cerebral cortex and in your nervous system of what it is like to write an absolute there is there are no better sales letters than this particular one. And if you would do that often on a repetitive basis it almost becomes second nature to you. I believe that if you did not take lessons from Jack Nicholas but just played with him every day and kind of just sort of modeled yourself after what he was doing. I'll bet you would play an awful lot better golf by the end of a particular golf season. The reason I ask you to read it and I thank you is you always want to read aloud what you have written and I mean even the little commas the address and everything you catch mistakes. Number one. And number two you catch parts of parts where you stumble. You need to remember that people will not be bored in print. If you lose them for a moment you have lost them forever. This is really not conversation. You can stumble and fumble and get off track in conversation and you have the person right there and you can go back and recover. When you're writing you can't. What you really want to make your writing like is you it's a magic that someone gets on a sliding board that's been greased this long sliding board and you want them to get off that sliding board and you want it to be a greased shoot right out to the other end with no bumps disturbing it whatsoever. Because if there are bumps and if there are sticking points then they may stop and you want it you want to make it effortless for them. Good writing is the hard good writing is very difficult to do. It is much harder than poor writing. The best writing in the United States is done in the National Enquirer. They are the highest paid writers of any publication in the country. Now granted what they write about is not profound from a cosmic point of view. But no one ever read a National Enquirer article that didn't understand what they what they were reading. And you know unless they couldn't speak or read English you can't miss it. And that's what you want to do. The first principle you might want to write this down the first thing you have to understand about good writing number one is you have something to say. Number two say it clearly. Those are the only two principles you really have to get right because if you get those two right you're home free. One time a guy paid me ten thousand dollars to write to rewrite a postcard. And my postcard didn't work as well as the one that he was already mailing. And because the offer was kind of scammy and my copy made it clear what you were getting. And you understood exactly what it was when I was finished with it. Now don't feel too sorry for that guy because I made him millions upon millions of dollars. But if you have something you know let's suppose for example that you had a child that was dying of some terrible disease. But you knew a way to prevent this. And you were explaining it to the child and you only had one chance to explain it. You would want to make absolutely certain that she got that. Would you not? And you wouldn't use complicated words or anything. No one ever looks down at you, anyone with intelligence doesn't, for writing too clearly. No one ever failed to order. I used to say no one ever failed to order because your advertising was too clear. Well provided that you have something worthwhile to sell they do not fail to order because it was too clear. They might fail to order because they didn't understand what it was that you were writing. You notice that when she read this she didn't stumble at all even though it wasn't her original letter. It because that letter was written and rewritten and rewritten over a period of 18 months till it was just flawless. Now we're going to talk about that letter because there's so many lessons to be learned in it. That letter was written in the 1960s and back then computer letters were just coming in to vogue. And all the letters before that said dear friend, dear resident, dear occupant. Just by virtue of the fact that this letter that you could put Dear Mr. Halbert in a letter would triple the response back then. Just making it personalized, triple the response as a rule of thumb. When you triple the response of your advertising you probably multiply the profit by 10 because the first big chunk of money comes in to pay your contribution to overhead. I want to tell you what the numbers were then. Back then postage was 6 cents and with that letter and postage and everything else it cost us $70 to put, I'm sorry, it cost us $100 to put a thousand letters in the mail. Those letters cost $100 bucks per thousand to put in the mail. They cost 10 cents a piece. Now today of course the postage would be $320 but that wasn't today. We got an average of a 7% response. 7% of a thousand is what? I will ask these simple questions without apology over and over. 70. Our average order was for two reports. Our average order was $3. That means for every 1000 letters we took in on average we took in $210. Our average cost to fill an order was 50 cents. We had 70 orders to fill. So we then had an additional, what's 70? It's $210. I'm sorry, 50 cents and 70 was $35. So our total cost were $135. Our total revenue was $210. 5 from 10 is 5, 3 from 10 is 7. That's it. We were making $75 profit back then on average from every 1000 letters that we mailed. Now the back end on that would produce 20 to 40% at a much higher number. So far that letter and close variations of that letter, they have mailed $600 million. But let's forget the more recent things. Let's talk about back when I was still there. My dear friend Dennis as you all know recently has died. But let's go back to where we had just mailed the first 30 million. Okay, we were making $75 for 1000 letters mailed. So we made $75,000 profit for every million letters that we mailed times 30. What is that times 30? Somebody tell me. What? 2,255,000 or something like that. Now right now I know how to do that same letter and exponentially increase the response of it. Because I wrote that letter nearly 30 years ago. I don't do that for the company, Halvord's Inc., which still bears my name, because my association with that company was with Dennis Haslinger and Dennis's dead. And ever since he died, I just figured the idea of continuing a relationship with him was just stupid. So I broke it off right after that. If you guys awake here? Anyway, so anyway, but that letter, the prices have changed, the sophistication of it has changed. And when I say 7%, that was an average. Some of those letters on certain names pulled 22%. We found out the more uncommon the name was. Now, if I wanted to go into the coat of arms business again, I could go in there and just like that dominate that industry. Now, what I have learned is that it wouldn't make any difference to me personally if I did that. I have learned that as far as money is concerned, my last name should be Teflon. Because it doesn't matter how much I make, none of it sticks to me. Really, it doesn't. I've made almost more money than anybody I know and I have less. And I have just come to reconcile the fact that I am forever to live in my comfort zone, which is to be in financial struggle. My body or my persona is a way station for the money on the way to somewhere else. I make millions and it just pops off me and goes somewhere else. I don't know what's going to happen if I can't write anymore. But someone said to me that that's good for all the rest of us, Gary, because it keeps you sharp. Maybe unconsciously I do that because I like to walk on the edge. Now, Terry, do they have their model letters yet? No. Okay. Here's what we're going to do. We're going to take 90 minutes for lunch. Now, I'm going to teach you right before your eyes, just before lunch, a major secret of success at Direct Now.
